title: Scaling Agentic Coding and Design with Multi-Agent Systems
subtitle: How multi-agent architectures and context resets enable autonomous full-stack development.
summary: This article explores novel AI engineering techniques used to overcome performance plateaus in autonomous coding and frontend design. By implementing generator-evaluator loops and structured context resets, developers can build complex applications that exceed the limits of single-model sessions. This shift from simple prompting to harness design marks a new era in reliable, high-quality AI output.

key_concepts.definition: The external environment and set of instructions that manage how an AI agent interacts with tools and tasks.
A multi-agent setup where one AI creates content and a separate AI critiques it to improve quality.
The tendency of an AI model to rush or degrade its performance as it approaches its memory limit.
Clearing an AI's memory window and starting fresh with a summary of previous work to maintain quality.
Structured documents that pass the necessary state and history from one AI session to the next.
The phenomenon where an AI confidently praises its own work even when the quality is mediocre.
Summarizing an ongoing conversation in place to save space without resetting the session.
An agent specialized in breaking down high-level requests into a list of smaller, manageable tasks.
Small, bite-sized tasks that an AI can complete reliably without losing focus.
An AI tuned specifically to find flaws and demand high standards from the generator's output.
Software that includes both the user interface and the server-side logic.
Qualities like beauty and polish that are hard to measure with binary code tests.

children.name: Multi-Agent Roles
children.description: The specific roles assigned to different AIs to ensure high-quality output.

children.name: Memory Management Strategies
children.description: Techniques for handling long tasks without hitting performance ceilings.

children.name: Design Quality Control
children.description: Methods for getting AI to produce polished frontend designs.

key_insights: AI agents suffer from 'context anxiety,' causing them to finish work prematurely as their memory fills up.
A standalone evaluator agent is significantly easier to tune for high standards than a generator agent is to tune for self-criticism.
Decomposing complex apps into 'tractable chunks' allows agents to remain coherent over multi-hour autonomous sessions.
The 'Ralph Wiggum' method uses external scripts to force agents into continuous iteration cycles rather than one-shot attempts.

analogies.explanation: Instead of one runner getting exhausted (context anxiety), a fresh runner takes over a clean track, carrying a clipboard of everything that happened before.

analogies.analogy: The Chef and the Food Critic.
analogies.explanation: A chef might think their own dish is great because they worked hard on it, but a dedicated critic provides the objective push needed to reach a Michelin-star level.
It captures the exact state of the world so that a new player (or agent) can pick up right where the last one left off without having to replay the whole game.
